input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]), **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12]), and **manufacturing processes** (Document [10]). The **physical twin** systems are characterized as follows:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating computational, biological, and physical components (e.g., embedded systems with biological feedback loops).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Sensors, actuators, and embedded controllers (e.g., IoT devices, microcontrollers).
     - **Biological Layer**: Biological entities (e.g., cells, tissues, or microbial systems) interacting with the physical environment.
     - **Cyber Layer**: Digital models, simulation engines, and AI-driven analytics for real-time monitoring and control.
   - **Operational Domain**: Critical infrastructure, healthcare (e.g., prosthetics, organ-on-a-chip), and environmental monitoring.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic Interactions**: Bidirectional coupling between physical/biological and digital layers via **real-time data exchange**.
     - **Adaptive Behavior**: Systems exhibit emergent properties requiring **closed-loop control** and **predictive modeling**.
     - **Scalability**: Designed for **modularity** to accommodate heterogeneous components (e.g., mixed hardware/software/bio systems).
     - **Safety-Critical**: Requires **fault tolerance** and **fail-safe mechanisms** due to potential biological/physical risks.

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [12])**:
   - **Type**: Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs), vehicle manufacturing lines, and powertrain systems.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Vehicles (e.g., electric/hybrid powertrains, battery systems), manufacturing robots, and supply chain logistics.
     - **Cyber Layer**: ECUs (Electronic Control Units), V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication, and cloud/edge computing platforms.
   - **Operational Domain**: Vehicle design, production, and in-service operation (e.g., **digital twin factories** for manufacturing).
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Real-Time Synchronization**: **Sub-millisecond latency** requirements for autonomous driving simulations (Document [8]).
     - **Multi-Domain Modeling**: Integration of **mechanical, electrical, thermal, and software** models (e.g., **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**).
     - **Lifecycle Management**: From **design-to-deployment** (e.g., **digital twin factories** for predictive maintenance in manufacturing).
     - **Regulatory Compliance**: Alignment with **ISO 23247** for **digital twin frameworks in manufacturing** (Document [10]).

3. **Manufacturing Systems (Document [10])**:
   - **Type**: Industrial manufacturing processes (e.g., CNC machines, assembly lines, additive manufacturing).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Machines, tools, and production assets with embedded sensors (e.g., **Industrial IoT (IIoT) devices**).
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Digital Thread** (ISO 23247) linking design, simulation, and production data.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Smart factories** with **predictive maintenance**, **quality control**, and **optimized production scheduling**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Digital Thread**: **Seamless data flow** from **CAD/CAM to real-time shop-floor monitoring** (ISO 23247-1).
     - **Simulation-Driven**: Use of **digital twins for virtual commissioning** to validate production lines before physical deployment.
     - **Energy Efficiency**: Optimization of **resource allocation** (e.g., power, materials) via **AI-driven analytics**.
     - **Standardized Interoperability**: Compliance with **ISO/IEC 30173** for **digital twin terminology** and **ISO 23247** for **framework principles**.

**Unifying Theme Across Domains**:
The physical twins are **heterogeneous, dynamic, and safety-critical**, requiring **real-time synchronization**, **multi-physics modeling**, and **closed-loop feedback** between digital and physical layers. The systems are often **edge-cloud hybrid** (Document [14]) to support **low-latency processing** and **data sovereignty**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems in the provided documents offer **domain-specific services** categorized into **monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, visualization, and decision support**. Below is a detailed breakdown:

1. **Monitoring Services (Documents [7], [8], [10], [11], [12])**:
   - **Real-Time Telemetry**: Continuous collection and aggregation of **sensor data** (e.g., vibration, temperature, pressure) from physical systems via **IIoT/Industrial IoT (IIoT) gateways**.
   - **Anomaly Detection**: **AI/ML-based** (e.g., **deep learning, time-series forecasting**) for identifying **deviations** in automotive powertrains (Document [8]) or manufacturing equipment (Document [10]).
   - **Health Monitoring**: **Predictive maintenance** for **battery systems** (Document [17]) and **aerospace components** (Document [16]) using **condition-based monitoring (CBM)**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Engineers**: For **fault diagnosis** and **root-cause analysis**.
     - **Operational Teams**: For **proactive maintenance scheduling**.
     - **Regulators**: For **compliance verification** (e.g., ISO 23247 audits).

2. **Optimization Services (Documents [9], [10], [14])**:
   - **Resource Allocation**: **AI-driven optimization** of **production schedules**, **energy consumption**, and **material usage** in smart factories (Document [10]).
   - **Trajectory Planning**: For **autonomous vehicles** (Document [8]), optimizing **route efficiency** and **energy consumption** via **digital twin simulations**.
   - **Process Parameters Tuning**: **Closed-loop control** of **manufacturing parameters** (e.g., cutting speeds, temperatures) to minimize defects (Document [9]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Manufacturing Managers**: For **cost reduction** and **throughput optimization**.
     - **Automotive OEMs**: For **fleet efficiency** improvements.

3. **Prediction Services (Documents [7], [11], [15])**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: **Machine learning models** (e.g., **LSTM networks**) trained on historical data to forecast **equipment failures** (e.g., **bearings, motors**) in automotive and industrial settings (Document [9]).
   - **Lifetime Estimation**: **Physics-based + data-driven** models for **battery degradation** (Document [17]) and **aerospace component wear** (Document [16]).
   - **Performance Forecasting**: **Digital twin simulations** for predicting **vehicle dynamics** (Document [8]) or **manufacturing yield** (Document [10]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Maintenance Teams**: For **preventive actions**.
     - **Researchers**: For **material science advancements** (e.g., battery chemistry).

4. **Control Services (Documents [7], [14], [16])**:
   - **Closed-Loop Automation**: **Real-time control** of **autonomous vehicles** (Document [8]) and **aerospace systems** (Document [16]) via **digital twin feedback**.
   - **Adaptive Control**: **Edge computing-based** (Document [14]) **control algorithms** for **cyber-biophysical systems** (e.g., adjusting biological feedback loops).
   - **Collision Avoidance**: **Space-based debris detection** (Document [16]) using **digital twin simulations** for **on-orbit maneuvering**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Robotics Engineers**: For **autonomous system deployment**.
     - **Aerospace Operators**: For **mission-critical adjustments**.

5. **Visualization Services (Documents [10], [15], [17])**:
   - **3D/4D Digital Models**: **Immersive visualization** of **manufacturing processes** (Document [10]) and **vehicle interiors** (Document [8]) using **Unreal Engine** or **Blender** for **AR/VR integration**.
   - **Dashboards**: **Real-time KPIs** (e.g., **OEE - Overall Equipment Effectiveness**) for **factory floors** (Document [10]).
   - **Interactive Simulations**: **What-if analysis** for **battery system failures** (Document [17]) and **aerospace scenarios** (Document [16]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Executives**: For **strategic decision-making**.
     - **Technicians**: For **troubleshooting**.

6. **Decision Support Services (Documents [12], [13], [15])**:
   - **Scenario Planning**: **Digital twin simulations** for **disaster response** (e.g., **cyber-physical crises**) and **automotive crash testing** (Document [8]).
   - **Policy Optimization**: **AI-driven recommendations** for **regulatory compliance** (e.g., **ISO 23247 adherence**) and **sustainability** (e.g., **carbon footprint reduction**).
   - **System-of-Twins Integration**: **Hierarchical DTs** (Document [13]) for **multi-scale decision-making** (e.g., **vehicle fleet + supply chain**).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Executive Leadership**: For **long-term strategy**.
     - **Regulatory Bodies**: For **standard compliance validation**.

**Service Enablers**:
- **AI/ML Integration**: **Reinforcement learning** (Document [15]) and **neural networks** for **predictive and control services**.
- **Real-Time Data Processing**: **Edge computing** (Document [14]) for **low-latency decision-making**.
- **Standardized APIs**: **ISO 23247-compliant** interfaces for **interoperability** (Document [10]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems described in the documents leverage a **heterogeneous stack** of **tools, frameworks, and technologies**, categorized by their roles in **data acquisition, simulation, processing, and deployment**. Below is a detailed breakdown:

1. **Data Acquisition & Integration (Documents [7], [10], [12], [14])**:
   - **Sensors & IoT Devices**:
     - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: **Modbus, OPC UA, MQTT** protocols for **real-time data collection** from **automotive and manufacturing systems** (Document [10]).
     - **Cyber-Biophysical Sensors**: **High-resolution biosensors** (e.g., **electrodes, optical sensors**) for **biological feedback loops** (Document [7]).
   - **Data Lakes/Streams**:
     - **Apache Kafka** or **AWS Kinesis** for **high-velocity data ingestion** (Document [14]).
     - **Relational/NoSQL Databases**: **PostgreSQL, MongoDB** for **structured/unstructured data storage** (Document [12]).

2. **Simulation & Modeling Engines (Documents [7], [10], [15], [16])**:
   - **Multi-Physics Simulation**:
     - **ANSYS, COMSOL, MATLAB/Simulink** for **finite element analysis (FEA)**, **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)**, and **electromagnetic simulations** (Document [10]).
     - **Digital Twin Factories**: **Virtual Commissioning** using **Siemens Plant Simulation** or **Rockwell Automation FactoryTalk** (Document [10]).
   - **AI/ML Simulation**:
     - **TensorFlow/PyTorch** for **deep learning-based predictions** (Document [15]).
     - **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)** for **cyber-biophysical modeling** (Document [7]).
   - **Aerospace-Specific**:
     - **NASA’s OpenMDAO** or **Siemens STAR-CCM+** for **space-based debris detection simulations** (Document [16]).

3. **Edge & Cloud Computing (Documents [14], [16])**:
   - **Edge Computing Frameworks**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson, AWS IoT Greengrass** for **on-premise processing** of **automotive and industrial data** (Document [14]).
     - **5G/6G Networks**: **Ultra-low latency (<10ms)** for **autonomous vehicle control** (Document [8]).
   - **Cloud Platforms**:
     - **AWS, Azure, Google Cloud** for **scalable DT deployments** (Document [12]).
     - **Hybrid Edge-Cloud**: **ISO 23247-compliant** architectures for **data sovereignty** (Document [14]).

4. **Development & Integration Tools (Documents [10], [12], [13])**:
   - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**:
     - **SysML, MATLAB Simulink, Capella** for **system architecture modeling** (Document [10]).
   - **Digital Twin Frameworks**:
     - **ISO 23247 Standard**: **Digital Thread** implementation for **manufacturing** (Document [10]).
     - **Digital Twin Maturity Model (DTM3)**: **Assessment frameworks** for **automotive and industrial adoption** (Document [9]).
   - **APIs & Standards**:
     - **OPC UA, RESTful APIs** for **interoperability** (Document [12]).
     - **Semantic Web Technologies**: **OWL, RDF** for **ontology-based DT modeling** (Document [11]).

5. **Visualization & User Interfaces (Documents [10], [15], [17])**:
   - **3D/AR/VR Tools**:
     - **Unreal Engine, Unity, Blender** for **immersive DT visualization** (Document [10]).
     - **Microsoft HoloLens** for **augmented reality maintenance guides** (Document [8]).
   - **Dashboards & Analytics**:
     - **Power BI, Tableau** for **KPI visualization** (Document [10]).
     - **Custom Web Portals** (e.g., **React.js + D3.js**) for **real-time monitoring**.

6. **AI & Machine Learning (Documents [7], [15])**:
   - **Predictive Analytics**:
     - **XGBoost, Random Forests** for **failure prediction** (Document [9]).
     - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** for **autonomous system optimization** (Document [15]).
   - **Digital Twin AI Framework**:
     - **AI Simulation by Digital Twins (ASDT)** (Document [15]) integrates **simulation + AI** for **closed-loop learning**.

7. **Security & Compliance (Documents [10], [12])**:
   - **Cybersecurity**:
     - **Zero Trust Architecture** for **DT data protection** (Document [12]).
     - **Blockchain** for **immutable audit trails** (Document [13]).
   - **Regulatory Standards**:
     - **ISO 23247** for **digital twin frameworks** (Document [10]).
     - **ISO/IEC 30173** for **terminology and concepts** (Document [11]).

**Domain-Specific Enablers**:
- **Automotive (Documents [8], [9])**:
  - **V2X Communication**: **5G, DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communication)** for **vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I)**.
  - **Digital Twin Factories**: **Siemens MindSphere** for **manufacturing process optimization**.
- **Aerospace (Document [16])**:
  - **Space-Based DTs**: **NASA’s OpenMDAO + STAR-CCM+** for **orbital mechanics simulations**.
- **Cyber-Biophysical (Document [7])**:
  - **Bio-Informatics Tools**: **CyVerse, Galaxy Project** for **biological data integration**.

**Implementation Challenges Addressed**:
- **Latency**: **Edge computing** (Document [14]) reduces **cloud dependency**.
- **Interoperability**: **ISO 23247 APIs** ensure **cross-vendor compatibility**.
- **Scalability**: **Kubernetes-based microservices** for **scalable DT deployments** (Document [12]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination. This temporal constraint is mandated due to hardware limitations and aligns with safety-critical vehicular application requirements. The system relies on strict time alignment between devices to ensure real-time data consistency, with no explicit mention of granularity for different services beyond the 100 ms threshold. Latency requirements are implicitly tied to this synchronization, as deviations could disrupt offloading and actuation workflows in the Physical Twin Entity (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary instances: *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247 by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities under the *Physical Twin Entity* (e.g., sensors, actuators, and compute resources), rather than adhering strictly to the standard’s *OME* (Operational Management Entity). Coordination between instances is achieved via **ROS2 packages** (one per entity), where each package contains ROS2 nodes implementing functionality. The *User Interface FE* is further split into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents, while the *Data Storage* component lacks a direct functional equivalent in the ISO 23247 framework. Hierarchical scope is implicit in the entity separation, with the *Digital Twin Entity* managing control/data mechanisms and the *Physical Twin Entity* handling real-time resource interactions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **component-based orchestration pattern** rooted in ISO 23247’s Reference Architecture (RA), with **ROS2** as the core middleware enabling modularity and interoperability. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS2 package, facilitating granular deployment across hardware components (e.g., *User Entity* on visualization systems, *Physical Twin Entity* on vehicle hardware). Resource management is distributed by entity, with the *Digital Twin Entity* overseeing data processing and control logic, while the *Physical Twin Entity* hosts resource-specific operations (e.g., sensor-actuator coordination). System-wide coordination relies on **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model** for inter-node communication and **clock synchronization** (≤100 ms) to align temporal operations. Deviations from ISO 23247 include the *Physical Twin Entity*’s expanded role beyond resource-specific tasks, potentially impacting functional ownership and engineering workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) with observable attributes. The system employs **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** to digitally represent OMEs, integrating **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** to simulate physical operations.

**Data sources** are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with OMEs to collect real-time operational data. The **Functional Entities (FEs)** within each entity handle specific tasks such as:
- **Data transformation** (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats),
- **Actuation control** (interfacing with physical actuators),
- **User interaction** (providing monitoring and control interfaces).

**Model relationships** are implicitly defined by the **entity-based decomposition**, where each DTE maintains a **bidirectional coupling** with its corresponding OME via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. The standard does not prescribe specific model types (e.g., CAD for geometry, physics-based simulations for behavior) but implies their integration into the DTE’s digital representation. **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is mentioned as a potential implementation choice for interfacing FEs, enabling standardized data exchange and model integration across the Digital Twin constellation.

**Data management approaches** are not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, but the architecture suggests centralized coordination via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, ensuring synchronized data flow between physical and digital domains. The system’s **information exchange** (Part 4 of ISO 23247) is implied to rely on structured protocols like OPC UA for consistency and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **ISO 23247** standard outlines foundational principles for **model fidelity** and **validity** in Digital Twin systems, though it does not provide explicit methodologies for validation, verification, or uncertainty quantification. The **entity-based reference architecture** implies that **model fidelity** is achieved through:
- **Bidirectional synchronization** between **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** and **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**,
- **Functional Entities (FEs)** ensuring accurate data transformation and actuation, though implementation specifics (e.g., error margins, correction algorithms) are not detailed.

**Uncertainty sources** are not explicitly addressed in the provided documents, but the standard’s emphasis on **real-time data collection** suggests that **sensor inaccuracies, communication delays, and model parameter drift** may introduce variability. **Error detection and correction mechanisms** are implied to rely on the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity’s** ability to reconcile discrepancies between physical and digital states, though no quantitative thresholds or automated correction protocols are specified.

**Quality assurance processes** are not described in the documents, but the **ISO 23247 framework** implies adherence to **standardized validation protocols** (e.g., compliance testing for OME representations) and **verification of FE functionality** (e.g., ensuring data transformation accuracy). The absence of concrete methods underscores the standard’s focus on **architectural principles** over operational validation techniques.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors) and their transformation into digital representations. **Data types transmitted** include raw point cloud data (LiDAR), vehicle state metrics (e.g., speed, acceleration), motor/servo actuation signals, and clustered/tracked object data derived from point cloud preprocessing.

**Communication protocols** for data transmission are not explicitly detailed, but the system leverages **ROS2** for intra-entity communication and **Wi-Fi with UDP** for inter-entity data propagation. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be high (e.g., LiDAR point clouds require real-time processing, typically ≥10Hz for autonomous systems).

**Event triggers** for data collection include continuous sensor polling (e.g., LiDAR scans) and discrete events (e.g., vehicle state changes). **Data quality assurance** is ensured through **preprocessing steps** executed by the *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking* SW component, which converts raw 2D LiDAR point clouds into structured object tracks and driving path projections. **Data integration mechanisms** involve the *Data Pre-Processing FE* (Functional Entity), which standardizes and aligns data streams via the **Data Translation component** (ISO 23247-compliant), performing serialization, unit conversions, and message type alignment. Visualization pre-processing (e.g., 3D rendering) further refines data for real-time display in the virtual cockpit (Fig. 3a).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the *Controlling FE* (Control Functional Entity), which translates digital commands into physical actuation. **Command types** include **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) inputs**, steering corrections, and motor actuation signals. **Control message formats** are standardized via the **Data Translation component**, ensuring compatibility across ROS2-based SW components.

**Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **legitimate software (SW) components** (referenced as *Lege SW* in the table) to enforce command integrity. **Feedback systems** are implied through real-time monitoring of vehicle state (e.g., speed, path deviation) and object tracks, which are fed back into the virtual twin for adaptive control adjustments.

**Conflict resolution** is not described, but the architecture suggests centralized control via the *Controlling FE*, which likely prioritizes high-level commands (e.g., ACC) over lower-level corrections. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred from the closed-loop nature of the system, where physical responses (e.g., motor/servo feedback) are reintegrated into the digital twin for consistency checks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** is built on a **multi-layered communication architecture**:
- **Intra-entity communication** occurs via **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2), enabling real-time data exchange between SW components (e.g., *Data Pre-Processing FE*, *Actuation FE*).
- **Inter-entity communication** (between Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity) uses **Wi-Fi with UDP** at the transport layer, ensuring low-latency data propagation.

**Network topology** is not explicitly detailed, but the architecture implies a **distributed system** with dedicated functional entities (FEs) for data processing, control, and visualization. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are critical for real-time applications; UDP ensures minimal latency but lacks reliability guarantees, suggesting redundancy may be handled via ROS2’s internal mechanisms or higher-layer protocols.

**Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not described, but the use of **ROS2** (a fault-tolerant framework) and **Wi-Fi** (with potential failover capabilities) implies inherent resilience. **Security protocols** are not mentioned, though UDP’s stateless nature could pose risks; no encryption or authentication layers are referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Key aspects such as **hosting platforms** (cloud/edge/on-premise), **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms**, or **deployment models** (e.g., containerized, microservices) are **Not in Document**.

However, the system’s reliance on **ROS2** and **Wi-Fi/UDP** suggests an **edge deployment model** (e.g., onboard vehicle computing) with potential cloud integration for data storage/reporting (as implied by *Reporting FE* and *Data Storage*). No specifics on hardware (e.g., GPU/CPU specs), software containers, or redundancy strategies are provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system is structured according to the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** with distinct phases and transitions. The lifecycle encompasses:

1. **Development Phase**:
   - **Design and Modeling**: The system is initialized with **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, which include physical entities such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes. Each OME is digitally represented by a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**, adhering to the ISO 23247 framework.
   - **Entity Composition**: The DTE is composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which are modular units responsible for specific tasks such as data collection, transformation, actuation, or user interfacing. FEs can be implemented using protocols like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability.
   - **Representation Types**: The DTE maintains a **digital representation** of the OME, synchronized with its physical counterpart. This representation evolves dynamically to reflect changes in the OME’s state or configuration.

2. **Operation Phase**:
   - **Data Collection and Control**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** interfaces with the OME to gather real-time or historical data, while also enabling remote control or monitoring of the physical system.
   - **User Interaction**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces (e.g., dashboards, APIs, or visualization tools) for operators to interact with the DTE, such as monitoring performance metrics or initiating control actions.

3. **Transition Management**:
   - Transitions between lifecycle stages (e.g., from design to operation) are governed by **entity-based workflows**, where each entity (OME, DTE, User Entity) adheres to predefined roles and responsibilities. The ISO 23247 standard does not specify automated transition mechanisms but emphasizes **modularity** and **interoperability** to facilitate seamless handoffs.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Governance is implied through the **standardized RA**, ensuring compliance with ISO 23247 principles. However, explicit governance frameworks (e.g., compliance audits or version control policies) are not detailed in the provided documents.

4. **Evolution Phase**:
   - The DT evolves through updates to the **Digital Twin Entity**, which may involve refining the digital representation (e.g., adding new FEs or improving data resolution) or adapting to changes in the OME (e.g., hardware upgrades or process modifications).
   - Evolution is driven by **feedback loops** between the physical and digital domains, where discrepancies or performance gaps trigger updates to the DTE’s logic or data models.

The lifecycle is **iterative**, with continuous synchronization between the physical OME and its digital counterpart to maintain accuracy and relevance.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** are governed by the **ISO 23247 reference architecture**, which outlines an **entity-based methodology** for development, testing, and continuous improvement. Key aspects include:

1. **Engineering and Development Methodologies**:
   - **Entity Decomposition**: The DT is decomposed into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** and their corresponding **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**, each managed by distinct **Functional Entities (FEs)**. FEs are responsible for tasks such as:
     - **Data Collection**: Gathering sensor data or telemetry from the OME (e.g., via **OPC UA** or other industrial protocols).
     - **Data Transformation**: Converting raw data into human-interpretable formats or standardized models (e.g., converting time-series data into performance metrics).
     - **Actuation**: Sending control commands to the OME (e.g., adjusting equipment parameters).
     - **User Interfacing**: Providing visualization, alerts, or decision support tools.
   - **Modular Implementation**: FEs can be implemented using **OPC UA** or other interoperable standards, enabling flexible integration with legacy or modern systems.

2. **Quality Assurance (QA) Practices**:
   - The ISO 23247 standard does not prescribe specific QA methodologies but emphasizes **entity-based validation**, where each FE’s functionality is verified against its designated role (e.g., ensuring data collection accuracy or control command reliability).
   - **Testing Approaches**: Testing is implied to occur during the **development phase**, where FEs are validated in isolation or integrated within the DTE. No specific testing frameworks (e.g., model-based testing or simulation tools) are mentioned.

3. **Version Control and Evolution Management**:
   - **Versioning**: While not explicitly detailed, the **iterative nature of the DT lifecycle** suggests version control mechanisms (e.g., tracking changes to the DTE’s digital representation or FE configurations) are required to manage updates.
   - **Evolution Strategies**: The DT evolves through:
     - **Adaptive Updates**: Modifying the DTE to reflect changes in the OME (e.g., adding new sensors or adjusting control logic).
     - **Feedback-Driven Refinement**: Using real-time or historical data to improve the DTE’s predictive or analytical capabilities (e.g., enhancing simulation models or anomaly detection algorithms).
     - **Modular Replacement**: Swapping or upgrading FEs without disrupting the entire DT system, leveraging the **entity-based RA** for scalability.

4. **Development Workflows**:
   - The process follows a **phased approach**, starting with **design and modeling** of OMEs and DTEs, progressing to **operation** (data collection/control), and culminating in **continuous evolution** based on performance and requirements.
   - **Interoperability**: The use of **OPC UA** and other standardized protocols ensures seamless data exchange between FEs, OMEs, and external systems (e.g., ERP or MES platforms).

No explicit mention of **Agile methodologies**, **CI/CD pipelines**, or **automated testing tools** is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages its **entity-based architecture** (as defined in **ISO 23247**) to provide **decision support capabilities**, though specific analytics or user interaction methods are not detailed beyond the standard’s framework. Key aspects include:

1. **Decision Support Capabilities**:
   - **Insights Generation**: The DT generates insights through:
     - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Aggregating data from **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, enabling operators to track performance metrics (e.g., equipment health, process efficiency).
     - **Predictive Analytics**: While not explicitly described, the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** is expected to incorporate **simulation models** or **machine learning** (implied by the need for adaptive decision-making) to forecast OME behavior or anticipate failures.
     - **Anomaly Detection**: Identifying deviations in OME operations by comparing real-time data against historical baselines or predefined thresholds.
   - **Analytics and Processing**: The **Functional Entities (FEs)** handle data processing tasks, such as:
     - **Data Transformation**: Converting raw sensor data into actionable insights (e.g., converting vibration signals into equipment wear metrics).
     - **Aggregation**: Consolidating data from multiple OMEs to derive high-level performance indicators (e.g., overall equipment effectiveness).
     - **Visualization**: Presenting insights via **User Entity interfaces**, though specific tools (e.g., dashboards or AR/VR platforms) are not mentioned.

2. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **User Interaction Methods**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces for:
     - **Monitoring**: Displaying real-time or historical data (e.g., dashboards for OME status).
     - **Control**: Allowing operators to issue commands to the OME (e.g., adjusting parameters via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**).
     - **Alerting**: Notifying users of critical events (e.g., equipment failures or process deviations).
   - **Confidence Measures**: The standard does not specify **confidence metrics** (e.g., uncertainty quantification or model accuracy scores) for recommendations. However, the **synchronization between the physical OME and its digital representation** implies that confidence is implicitly tied to data fidelity and model validity.

3. **Technical Foundations**:
   - The **DTE’s predictive or analytical capabilities** rely on:
     - **Simulation Models**: Dynamically updating digital representations to reflect OME behavior.
     - **Data-Driven Logic**: Using historical and real-time data to inform decisions (e.g., maintenance scheduling).
   - **Interoperability**: The use of **OPC UA** ensures seamless data flow between FEs, enabling cohesive decision-making across the DT system.

No explicit mention of **advanced analytics tools** (e.g., Python-based libraries, AI/ML frameworks), **confidence thresholds**, or **automated decision-making algorithms** is provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, which provides a comprehensive **compliance framework** and **interoperability standards**. Key aspects include:

1. **Relevant Industry Standards**:
   - **ISO 23247**: The foundational standard for the DT system, structured into four parts:
     - **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**: Defines an **entity-based RA** with four core entities:
       - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Physical items with observable attributes (e.g., equipment, processes).
       - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Handles data acquisition and OME control.
       - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: The digital representation of the OME.
       - **User Entity**: Provides user interfaces for interaction.
     - **Part 3 (Digital Representation)**: Specifies how OMEs are digitally modeled (e.g., using **OPC UA** or other standardized formats).
     - **Part 4 (Information Exchange)**: Ensures interoperability between entities via protocols like **OPC UA**.

2. **Specifications Followed**:
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Mentioned as a potential implementation choice for **Functional Entities (FEs)**, enabling standardized communication between the DT system and industrial devices or systems.
   - **Modularity**: The DT system is designed with **modular FEs**, allowing independent development, testing, and deployment of components.
   - **Entity-Based Design**: Each entity (OME, DTE, etc.) adheres to predefined roles and responsibilities, ensuring consistency and scalability.

3. **Certification Requirements**:
   - The ISO 23247 standard does not explicitly outline **certification processes** for DT systems. However, compliance with the standard implies adherence to its **reference architecture and information exchange guidelines**, which may be audited for interoperability or functional correctness.
   - **Implicit Compliance**: Systems following ISO 23247 are expected to meet **interoperability**, **data accuracy**, and **functional integrity** requirements, though formal certification bodies are not referenced.

4. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **OPC UA**: The primary standard for data exchange between the DT system and external systems (e.g., ERP, MES, or IoT platforms).
   - **Standardized Data Models**: The DTE’s digital representation of OMEs must align with **ISO 23247’s specifications**, ensuring consistency across manufacturing domains.
   - **Protocol Agnosticism**: While OPC UA is highlighted, the standard allows flexibility in choosing other **industrial communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, Profibus) for specific use cases.

No mention of **additional certifications** (e.g., IEC 62443 for cybersecurity), **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO 9001), or **vendor-specific standards** is provided.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, are primarily governed by **ISO 26262-1:2018** for functional safety in road vehicles and **ISO 15622:2018** for adaptive cruise control systems. Key technical measures include:

1. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262-1:2018)**:
   - Adherence to a **safety lifecycle** (ASIL decomposition, hazard analysis, and risk assessment) to ensure system reliability and fault tolerance.
   - Implementation of **automatic diagnostics** (e.g., watchdog timers, error detection codes) to detect and respond to hardware/software failures in real-time.
   - **Safety mechanisms** such as fail-safe modes, redundancy, and **safety-critical software development** following MISRA C/C++ guidelines to mitigate risks.
   - **Cybersecurity integration** aligned with ISO 21434 (though not explicitly mentioned, implied via safety-critical system design).

2. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ISO 15622:2018)**:
   - **Performance requirements** mandate **cybersecurity resilience** to prevent unauthorized access or manipulation of control systems, ensuring **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA)** of critical data.
   - **Access control mechanisms** (e.g., role-based authentication) to restrict system modifications, with **audit trails** for compliance verification.
   - **Threat mitigation** via **secure communication protocols** (e.g., encrypted V2X data exchanges) to protect against spoofing or denial-of-service attacks.
   - **Fail-safe mechanisms** to ensure graceful degradation (e.g., transitioning to manual control) if system integrity is compromised.

3. **General Safety-Critical System Considerations**:
   - **Risk assessment** using **ISO 26262’s ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** framework to classify system components based on severity, exposure, and controllability.
   - **Hardware redundancy** (e.g., dual-core processors, NVRAM) to ensure continuous operation despite single-point failures.
   - **Software integrity** via **static/dynamic analysis tools** (e.g., Coverity, Polyspace) to detect vulnerabilities during development.
   - **Physical security** (e.g., tamper-resistant enclosures) to prevent unauthorized physical access to safety-critical components.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
